definitions: a departure from what is normal or expected
example: Her calm response was an aberration from the angry outbursts that had defined her throughout the proceedings — everyone in the room noticed it.

definitions: moderate and self-disciplined, especially in eating or drinking
example: While everyone around him ordered dessert and wine, he remained abstemious, choosing only water and a small salad.

definitions: an award, honor, or expression of praise
example: Winning the industry's highest prize was the greatest accolade of her thirty-year career — the one she had stopped expecting.

definitions: keen judgment and practical understanding
example: His financial acumen was evident when he identified the undervalued stock months before the market caught on.

definitions: skillful, clever, and quick in action or thought
example: She was adroit at navigating difficult conversations, steering them toward resolution before tensions could escalate.

definitions: friendly and easy to talk to
example: Despite being the most senior partner in the firm, he remained affable and approachable to every junior associate.

definitions: brisk and cheerful readiness
example: When the fire alarm sounded, the staff evacuated the building with impressive alacrity — not a single person needed to be told twice.

definitions: to make something less severe
example: The new medication did little to alleviate her pain, which continued through the night despite the increased dose.

definitions: to combine into a unified whole
example: The two rival newspapers decided to amalgamate, combining their newsrooms and resources under a single masthead.

definitions: having mixed or conflicting feelings
example: She was ambivalent about taking the promotion — excited by the opportunity but reluctant to leave the team she had spent years building.

definitions: to improve or make better
example: Adding extra staff did little to ameliorate the underlying process failures that were causing the delays in the first place.

definitions: belonging to a time different from the one being discussed
example: Insisting on fax confirmations in the age of digital contracts struck most clients as decidedly anachronistic.

definitions: comparable in certain respects
example: The heart's role in the body is analogous to a pump in a hydraulic system — both circulate fluid to keep the whole mechanism alive.

definitions: something that deviates from the usual pattern
example: Every reading matched the expected range except one striking anomaly that no one in the lab could immediately explain.

definitions: to provoke hostility or opposition
example: Rather than resolve the conflict, his dismissive tone served only to antagonize the already frustrated customers.

definitions: the direct opposite of something
example: Her open, collaborative management style was the antithesis of her predecessor's secretive, top-down approach.

definitions: self-confidence and composure
example: Despite the projector failing mid-presentation, she continued without notes and handled the setback with remarkable aplomb.

definitions: of doubtful authenticity, though widely repeated
example: The story of him swimming across the harbour on a dare is almost certainly apocryphal, but it gets retold every year.

definitions: understood by only a few; obscure
example: The tax code is riddled with arcane provisions that even seasoned accountants struggle to interpret.

definitions: able to express ideas clearly and effectively
example: She was so articulate in explaining the complex policy that even journalists who had covered the topic for years left with a clearer understanding.

definitions: showing careful and persistent effort
example: Through assiduous practice — four hours every day for three years — he transformed himself from a novice into a virtuoso.

definitions: quick to notice and understand things accurately
example: An astute observer would have noticed the subtle shift in the CEO's language weeks before the strategy change was announced.

definitions: bold, daring, and sometimes shockingly confident
example: Landing a probe on a comet travelling at 84,000 miles per hour was an audacious feat that most scientists had considered impossible.

definitions: severe in manner or simple in style
example: Life in the monastery was deliberately austere — bare walls, simple food, no visitors, and no contact with the outside world.

definitions: extreme greed for wealth
example: His avarice was so consuming that even after amassing a fortune he continued underpaying his workers and cutting corners on safety.

definitions: a cause of great distress or annoyance
example: Procrastination had been the bane of his academic career, costing him opportunities he could never recover.

definitions: to give a false impression of
example: Her cheerful voice belied the exhaustion she felt after three consecutive all-night shifts.

definitions: kind and well meaning
example: The benevolent donor funded the entire hospital wing anonymously, asking for nothing in return and refusing all public recognition.

definitions: gentle, harmless, or kindly
example: What had appeared on the scan as a worrying mass turned out to be entirely benign — harmless tissue that required no treatment.

definitions: showing a casual and cheerful indifference
example: She seemed blithe about the looming deadline, chatting in the hallway while her colleagues worked in near panic.

definitions: concise and exact use of words
example: The general's orders were models of brevity — each one a single sentence that left no room for misinterpretation.

definitions: a harsh mixture of sounds
example: Opening the door released a cacophony of barking, clattering dishes, and competing music from every room in the house.

definitions: truthful and straightforward
example: In a refreshingly candid interview, the CEO admitted that the product launch had been a costly and avoidable mistake.

definitions: given to sudden and unpredictable changes
example: The capricious weather shifted from blazing sun to hailstorm and back again within the same afternoon.

definitions: to express strong disapproval of
example: The committee voted to formally censure the official for using public funds for personal travel — the first such reprimand in a decade.

definitions: careful to consider possible risks before acting
example: Knowing the deal could collapse over a single poorly chosen word, the negotiator was extremely circumspect in every statement she made.

definitions: clear, logical, and convincing
example: The lawyer presented a cogent argument — each point building logically on the last — that left the jury little room for reasonable doubt.

definitions: logical, consistent, and easy to follow
example: After three sleepless days, he was barely coherent, answering questions with fragments that trailed off before reaching any conclusion.

definitions: too satisfied to see the need for improvement
example: Years of unchallenged dominance had left the company complacent, and they were completely unprepared when a nimble competitor emerged.

definitions: brief but comprehensive
example: His report was concise without sacrificing substance — twenty focused pages where others had submitted hundreds.

definitions: to agree with an opinion or decision
example: After reviewing the evidence independently, all three engineers concurred that the structure was unsafe and should be closed immediately.

definitions: to combine two ideas as if they were one
example: It is important not to conflate correlation with causation — two things occurring together does not mean one caused the other.

definitions: careful, responsible, and thorough
example: She was a conscientious employee who double-checked every figure and never submitted work she was not fully satisfied with.

definitions: general agreement among a group
example: After three hours of debate, the committee finally reached a consensus — not unanimous agreement, but broad enough to move forward.

definitions: likely to cause argument or disagreement
example: The redistricting proposal was deeply contentious, drawing angry protests from residents on both sides of the new boundary.

definitions: a confusing and difficult problem
example: How to encourage innovation while maintaining strict security standards was a conundrum the team had been wrestling with for months.

definitions: abundant in quantity
example: She took copious notes throughout the lecture, filling four notebooks and still worrying she had missed something.

definitions: to confirm or support with evidence
example: A second witness came forward to corroborate the account, confirming the same timeline and sequence of events independently.

definitions: believable and trustworthy
example: The alibi was credible enough that investigators couldn't rule him out, but thin enough that they kept watching.

definitions: having a meaning that is mysterious or obscure
example: She left a cryptic note on his desk — just three numbers and a symbol — that he spent the rest of the day trying to decode.

definitions: hasty and not thorough
example: A cursory reading of the contract would have missed the clause buried in the footnotes that ultimately cost them the deal.

definitions: a scarcity or lack of something
example: There was a dearth of experienced candidates for the role, forcing the company to consider promoting someone from within.

definitions: a sudden and disastrous failure
example: The launch was a complete debacle — servers crashed, orders went missing, and customers flooded social media with complaints within the first hour.

definitions: respectful submission to another's judgment
example: In deference to the senior members of the committee, the younger delegates held their objections until asked directly.

definitions: harmful or damaging
example: The deleterious effects of the chemical on nearby vegetation became apparent within weeks of its introduction into the soil.

definitions: to describe or explain precisely
example: The contract carefully delineated each party's responsibilities, leaving no room for disputes about who was accountable for what.

definitions: reserved, modest, and quiet
example: She was demure at the interview, speaking softly and choosing her words carefully — composure that some mistook for shyness.

definitions: to unfairly criticize and belittle
example: He had a habit of denigrating his colleagues' contributions, claiming credit for shared successes while distancing himself from any failures.

definitions: to mock or ridicule
example: Critics derided the film as shallow and derivative, yet audiences loved it and it became one of the year's biggest box office successes.

definitions: empty, bleak, and lonely
example: They arrived to find the town desolate — every building abandoned, every street empty, the whole place silent as a ruin.

definitions: skillful with the hands or with actions
example: The surgeon's dexterous hands completed the intricate repair in half the time her colleagues would have needed.

definitions: intended to teach, sometimes too directly
example: The novel's didactic tone — characters who exist mainly to illustrate moral lessons — made it feel more like a sermon than a story.

definitions: lacking confidence; shy
example: Too diffident to ask for help, he spent weeks struggling alone with a problem that a single conversation could have resolved in an hour.

definitions: hardworking and careful
example: Her diligent research — combing through decades of archived records — uncovered a pattern no one had previously noticed.

definitions: to perceive or recognize clearly
example: Even from across the room she could discern the tension between them from the clipped exchanges and careful avoidance of eye contact.

definitions: fundamentally different in kind
example: The team was made up of people with disparate backgrounds — a former chef, a structural engineer, and a retired diplomat.

definitions: to spread information widely
example: The organisation used its network to rapidly disseminate the safety information to tens of thousands of people within hours.

definitions: easy to manage or teach
example: Once trained, the horse was completely docile, responding to the lightest touch of the reins without resistance.

definitions: asserting opinions as unquestionably true
example: His dogmatic insistence that there was only one correct approach prevented the team from exploring solutions that might have worked far better.

definitions: doubtful or not to be relied on
example: The investment sounded lucrative, but several details made her dubious about the promoter's true intentions.

definitions: cheerful and full of energy
example: His ebullient energy was infectious — within minutes of arriving at a gathering, he had everyone laughing and talking.

definitions: drawing ideas or style from many sources
example: Her apartment reflected her eclectic tastes: mid-century furniture alongside vintage maps, African textiles, and contemporary photography.

definitions: the ability to produce the intended result
example: Clinical trials confirmed the drug's efficacy, showing it reduced symptoms in over eighty percent of patients within two weeks.

definitions: fluent, persuasive, and expressive
example: Her eloquent tribute captured the complexities of her mentor's character in a way that moved the entire room to tears.

definitions: to make clear by explanation
example: The diagram helped elucidate what the text alone had left confusing — suddenly the whole process made clear sense.

definitions: difficult to find, catch, or understand
example: Sleep was elusive that night, retreating each time she reached for it and leaving her staring at the ceiling until dawn.

definitions: to imitate in order to equal or surpass
example: Younger players tried to emulate his technique, studying footage of his form frame by frame to understand what set him apart.

definitions: a person or thing that is mysterious or puzzling
example: Even those who had worked with him for years found him an enigma — warm and generous in person, impossible to read.

definitions: deep-seated hostility
example: The enmity between the two families had persisted so long that no one could remember what had first caused it.

definitions: great wickedness or the extreme seriousness of something bad
example: It took months before the full enormity of what had happened — the scale of the loss, the lives affected — began to sink in.

definitions: to weaken or drain of energy
example: Three back-to-back transatlantic flights had completely enervated her, leaving her too drained to hold a clear thought.

definitions: to cause or bring about
example: The new transparency policy engendered a level of trust between management and staff that had never existed before.

definitions: lasting for a very short time
example: Social media trends are profoundly ephemeral — something that dominates every feed on Monday is entirely forgotten by Thursday.

definitions: a perfect example of a quality or type
example: She was the epitome of grace under pressure, never raising her voice even when everything around her was falling apart.

definitions: mental calmness, especially in a difficult situation
example: He accepted the devastating diagnosis with such equanimity — no anger, no denial — that the doctor who delivered it was left shaken.

definitions: to use vague language to avoid committing to a position
example: When asked directly whether the funds had been misused, the treasurer equivocated, offering vague reassurances rather than a direct answer.

definitions: having extensive knowledge gained from study
example: The professor was deeply erudite, moving effortlessly between ancient philosophy, modern physics, and the history of classical music.

definitions: intended for or understood by a small specialized group
example: The conference attracted specialists in an esoteric branch of computational linguistics that most academics had never encountered.

definitions: the guiding beliefs or character of a group or culture
example: The company's ethos — that quality should never be sacrificed for speed — was visible in everything from its hiring decisions to its products.

definitions: a mild word used in place of one that may seem harsh
example: "Downsizing" is a euphemism for layoffs, designed to make mass job losses sound like a routine and painless administrative adjustment.

definitions: to show clearly
example: His trembling hands and flushed face evinced a nervousness that his measured words were working hard to conceal.

definitions: to make a problem worse
example: Adding more traffic signals actually exacerbated the congestion rather than relieving it, creating new bottlenecks at every intersection.

definitions: to clear from blame
example: The surveillance footage exculpated the suspect entirely, showing he was on the other side of the city when the incident occurred.

definitions: serving as an excellent model
example: Her exemplary record — not a single formal complaint in twelve years — made her the obvious and uncontested choice for the role.

definitions: stated clearly and in detail
example: The contract was explicit about the payment schedule, leaving no room for misunderstanding about when each instalment was due.

definitions: to make a process happen sooner or faster
example: They hired a specialist to expedite the permit process, which had been stalled for months in bureaucratic delays.

definitions: to praise enthusiastically
example: The eulogy extolled his generosity, recounting dozens of quiet acts of kindness that most people in the room had never heard about.

definitions: irrelevant or unnecessary
example: She cut every extraneous detail from the report, reducing forty pages to twelve without losing any substance.

definitions: treating serious issues with joking humor
example: His facetious suggestion that they solve the budget crisis by selling the building got a laugh, but it quickly became clear he wasn't entirely joking.

definitions: to make an action or process easier
example: The mediator's role was to facilitate dialogue between the two sides, not to take positions or advocate for either party.

definitions: based on mistaken belief or flawed reasoning
example: The argument rested on a fallacious premise — that because two events had coincided, one must have caused the other.

definitions: very attentive to detail and accuracy
example: She was fastidious about her research, verifying every claim through multiple independent sources before committing it to the page.

definitions: to understand after much thought
example: He simply could not fathom why anyone would voluntarily leave a secure position without something better already in place.

definitions: practical and capable of being done
example: Given the timeline and available budget, a full renovation was not feasible, so they focused on the most critical repairs.

definitions: having or showing intense feeling
example: She was a fervent advocate for early childhood education, citing its lifelong impact in every address she gave.

definitions: shockingly obvious and offensive
example: Parking in a fire lane during an active emergency was a flagrant disregard for the safety of everyone in the building.

definitions: overly elaborate in style or language
example: His florid prose — every sentence dense with adjectives and elaborate metaphors — exhausted readers before they reached the midpoint.

definitions: direct and honest in speech and behavior
example: He was always forthright about the company's challenges, even when the news was unwelcome — which is precisely why people trusted him.

definitions: happening by chance, often in a helpful way
example: Running into her former professor at the airport proved fortuitous — that chance encounter led directly to the collaboration that defined her career.

definitions: careful about spending money or resources
example: Her frugal habits — packing lunch, buying secondhand, shopping only from a list — allowed her to save more than most people earning twice her salary.

definitions: to shock or spur into action
example: The documentary galvanized the public, transforming a slow-moving environmental concern into an urgent national conversation.

definitions: excessively talkative
example: The garrulous cab driver offered an unbroken commentary on the city, its politics, and its history for the entire forty-minute ride.

definitions: sociable and fond of company
example: Naturally gregarious, she could walk into a room full of strangers and leave an hour later with a dozen new acquaintances.

definitions: overused and lacking freshness
example: The script was full of hackneyed phrases — clichés so worn smooth that the audience could predict the next line before it was spoken.

definitions: a sign that something is about to happen
example: The sudden departure of three senior executives was widely seen as a harbinger of deeper problems to come.

definitions: to pay careful attention to
example: Had they heeded the early warning signs — the minor tremors, the rising groundwater — many casualties might have been avoided.

definitions: uniform in composition or character
example: The neighbourhood had once been homogeneous in character, but over two decades it had become one of the most diverse communities in the city.

definitions: a person who challenges widely accepted beliefs
example: An uncompromising iconoclast, she spent her career challenging assumptions that everyone else in her field had long accepted as settled.

definitions: peculiar to an individual
example: His idiosyncratic filing system — organised by colour and intuition rather than any recognisable logic — somehow worked perfectly for him.

definitions: about to happen very soon
example: With dark clouds massing on the horizon and the barometric pressure dropping rapidly, a storm was clearly imminent.

definitions: unchanging over time
example: The laws of thermodynamics are immutable — no amount of engineering ingenuity can change them, only work within their constraints.

definitions: fair and not favoring one side
example: The judge was scrupulously impartial, applying the same standard to both sides regardless of public pressure or media attention.

definitions: absolutely necessary or urgent
example: With supplies running low and weather closing in fast, finding shelter before nightfall was absolutely imperative.

definitions: having very little money
example: Despite his impecunious circumstances — sharing a single room, eating once a day — he refused every offer of financial help.

definitions: rude, disrespectful, or not relevant
example: The reporter's impertinent questions, designed to provoke rather than inform, drew sharp rebukes from every member of the panel.

definitions: acting quickly without enough thought
example: Her impetuous decision to quit her job and move abroad without a plan led to three years of adventure and one spectacular failure.

definitions: suggested without being directly stated
example: There was an implicit understanding that certain topics — the estrangement, the years of silence — would not be raised at the reunion.

definitions: to create or act without preparation
example: When the main speaker cancelled an hour before the event, the organiser had to improvise an entirely new programme on the spot.

definitions: not intentional
example: The disclosure was inadvertent — a document attached to the wrong email — but the damage it caused was very real.

definitions: clear, sharp, and intelligently analytical
example: Her incisive questioning cut through weeks of evasion and finally forced the committee to confront the central issue directly.

definitions: unwilling or unable to believe something
example: She stared at him with an incredulous expression, unable to believe that anyone could genuinely hold such an opinion.

definitions: habitually lazy
example: After years of indolent habits — sleeping late, avoiding effort — he found himself entirely unprepared when circumstances finally demanded otherwise.

definitions: hardworking and productive
example: The industrious young engineer stayed late every night not because she was required to, but because she found the problems genuinely fascinating.

definitions: too great or extreme to be described in words
example: The view from the cliff at sunrise was ineffable — every attempt to describe it later in words felt hopelessly inadequate.

definitions: lacking skill or competence
example: His inept handling of the negotiation — making concessions before being asked and showing his hand too early — cost the company a far better deal.

definitions: impossible to stop or prevent
example: The inexorable advance of the wildfire gave residents less than two hours to gather what they could and evacuate.

definitions: to draw a conclusion from evidence
example: From the empty desks, the darkened offices, and the silence, she could infer that everyone had already left for the day.

definitions: innocent, sincere, and straightforward
example: He was so ingenuous that he took every promise at face value, never imagining that someone might have a hidden motive.

definitions: to restrain or hold back
example: Fear of public embarrassment can inhibit learning — students afraid to be wrong rarely take the risks that lead to real understanding.

definitions: present from birth or naturally possessed
example: Some musicians seem to possess an innate sense of rhythm that exists before any formal training begins.

definitions: impossible to satisfy
example: Her insatiable curiosity led her to read everything she could find on any subject that caught her interest, however briefly.

definitions: spreading harm gradually and subtly
example: The most insidious aspect of the misinformation campaign was how slowly and quietly it eroded public trust over several years.

definitions: honesty and strong moral principles
example: Her integrity was tested when she discovered the error in the data — reporting it would cost the company a major contract, but she reported it anyway.

definitions: occurring at irregular intervals
example: The intermittent signal — strong for a few seconds, then gone entirely — made it impossible to maintain a reliable connection.

definitions: fearless and adventurous
example: The intrepid correspondent spent three months embedded with aid workers in one of the world's most dangerous conflict zones.

definitions: easy to understand or use without instruction
example: The interface was so intuitive that most users needed no instructions at all — they simply knew what to do next.

definitions: to accustom someone to something difficult
example: Years of working night shifts had inured her to the loneliness and fatigue that new hires found so difficult to endure.

definitions: firmly established by long habit
example: An inveterate traveller, he had visited over ninety countries and showed no sign of slowing down at seventy-two.

definitions: happening in a way that is opposite of what was expected
example: It was ironic that the man who had campaigned most loudly against development ended up selling his land to the very company he had opposed.

definitions: showing good judgment and restraint
example: A judicious selection of examples — neither too many nor too few — made the argument persuasive without feeling padded.

definitions: to place side by side for comparison or contrast
example: The exhibition juxtaposed photographs of the city taken in 1920 and 2020, making the transformation feel both vast and immediate.

definitions: highly developed, sharp, or eager
example: She had a keen eye for inconsistency — the kind of small discrepancy in a document that others would scroll past without noticing.

definitions: using very few words
example: His laconic response — a single raised eyebrow and the word "interesting" — told her everything she needed to know about his opinion.

definitions: deserving praise
example: Their effort to provide meals to every child in the district over the summer, entirely through private fundraising, was genuinely laudable.

definitions: present but not yet visible or active
example: The latent talent the coach had spotted in him during that first informal session took years of patient work to fully develop.

definitions: to praise highly
example: The report lauded the paramedics for their swift response, crediting their actions with saving three lives at the scene.

definitions: sluggish and lacking energy
example: He felt lethargic for days after the illness passed — able to go through the motions but incapable of genuine focus.

definitions: lightness of manner or humor
example: A touch of levity from the chair broke the tension after three hours of difficult debate, and the room visibly relaxed.

definitions: clear and easy to understand
example: Even in her nineties, her mind remained lucid — her recall sharp, her arguments clear, her wit entirely intact.

definitions: producing a great deal of profit
example: What had begun as a weekend hobby became surprisingly lucrative once she started selling her work online.

definitions: generous and forgiving, especially toward a rival
example: Despite the years of rivalry and the bitterness of the campaign, he was magnanimous in victory, praising his opponent's long service.

definitions: easily influenced or shaped
example: Young children are far more linguistically malleable than adults — they absorb a second language without the same conscious effort.

definitions: an official order or requirement
example: The landslide result gave the new government a clear mandate to pursue the reforms it had promised throughout the campaign.

definitions: small in importance or effect
example: The improvement in test scores was marginal — just enough to claim progress, but not enough to suggest the programme was working.

definitions: lacking in quantity or quality
example: The meager salary barely covered rent and groceries, leaving nothing at the end of each month and no prospect of saving.

definitions: showing great attention to detail
example: He was meticulous in his preparation — rehearsing every answer, researching every panelist, and preparing for questions no one else anticipated.

definitions: to make less severe or serious
example: Planting trees along the roofline helped mitigate the heat island effect in the surrounding streets during summer months.

definitions: a small quantity of something
example: If he had shown even a modicum of remorse — any acknowledgment of what he had done — the outcome might have been different.

definitions: to adjust or regulate in degree or tone
example: A skilled speaker modulates tone and pace — slowing for emphasis, quickening for energy — rather than speaking in a flat monotone throughout.

definitions: ordinary and lacking excitement
example: The most mundane details of daily life — grocery lists, bus schedules, small arguments — accumulated over decades into something that felt like a world.

definitions: vague, unclear, or ill-defined
example: The plan was nebulous at best — full of aspirational language and vague timelines but almost entirely short on specifics.

definitions: wicked or criminal
example: The investigation eventually exposed the full extent of his nefarious scheme, which had defrauded hundreds of investors over ten years.

definitions: so small as to be unimportant
example: The added cost was negligible compared to the time it would save, so the committee approved the upgrade without debate.

definitions: appearing casually calm and unconcerned
example: He shrugged with a nonchalant air, as though losing the account were a minor inconvenience rather than a crisis.

definitions: new and original
example: The approach was entirely novel — no one in the field had thought to apply the technique this way, which is precisely why it worked.

definitions: a subtle difference in meaning or expression
example: A good translator doesn't just convert words — they preserve the nuance of tone, implication, and cultural context that gives language its full meaning.

definitions: stubbornly refusing to change
example: Despite overwhelming evidence that the plan was failing, he remained obdurate, refusing to hear any suggestion of adjustment.

definitions: not influenced by personal feelings or opinions
example: As a close friend of one of the parties, she knew she could not be fully objective and declined to serve on the review panel.

definitions: no longer produced or used; out of date
example: Within a decade, the technology that had seemed so advanced had become obsolete, replaced by systems that were cheaper and far more capable.

definitions: stubborn and difficult to persuade
example: His obstinate refusal to seek medical attention turned what might have been a minor issue into a serious and prolonged one.

definitions: to make something unclear or confusing
example: The committee accused the agency of deliberately obfuscating the data — presenting figures in a way designed to mislead rather than inform.

definitions: indirect or not expressed plainly
example: Rather than addressing the accusation directly, he gave an oblique response that seemed designed to leave maximum room for later interpretation.

definitions: excessively eager to please or obey
example: The obsequious assistant agreed with every opinion the director expressed, never offering a contrary view even when it might have helped.

definitions: slow to understand or not sharp
example: He was being deliberately obtuse — pretending not to understand a point that every other person in the room had grasped immediately.

definitions: suggesting that something bad is likely to happen
example: The board meeting had an ominous atmosphere from the start — no agenda circulated, no refreshments, and several faces no one recognised.

definitions: hard to understand; not transparent
example: The contract language was so opaque that three lawyers read it and arrived at three different conclusions about what it actually required.

definitions: well chosen for the circumstances
example: She waited for the most opportune moment — when he was relaxed, in a good mood, and away from the office — before raising the subject.

definitions: elaborately decorated or detailed
example: The ballroom was almost overwhelmingly ornate — every surface gilded, every ceiling painted, every doorframe carved with figures and foliage.

definitions: appearing to be true but possibly not so
example: His ostensible reason for the visit was to deliver papers, but it quickly became clear he was there to gather information.

definitions: showy in a way meant to impress
example: The party was so ostentatious — ice sculptures, a live orchestra, fireworks at midnight — that most guests felt uncomfortable rather than impressed.

definitions: a typical example or model
example: The discovery forced scientists to abandon the dominant paradigm and rebuild their theoretical framework almost entirely from scratch.

definitions: a statement or situation that seems self-contradictory but may be true
example: It is a genuine paradox that the countries with the most advanced medical systems often have among the least healthy populations.

definitions: extremely unwilling to spend money or resources
example: He was so parsimonious that he drove forty minutes out of his way to buy petrol that was two cents cheaper.

definitions: strongly supporting one side or group
example: The committee's findings were dismissed by critics as partisan — shaped by political loyalty rather than any independent analysis.

definitions: to treat someone as less intelligent or important
example: She hated the way he patronized younger colleagues, explaining obvious concepts slowly and carefully as though they were incapable of understanding.

definitions: deep in serious thought
example: He sat at the window in a pensive mood, turning the problem over in his mind without yet being ready to speak about it.

definitions: quick to notice and understand things
example: Her perceptive observation — that the client was answering questions they hadn't been asked while avoiding those they had — changed the direction of the inquiry.

definitions: done with minimal effort or interest
example: The inspection was perfunctory at best — a ten-minute walkthrough with no documentation, no tests, and no follow-up questions.

definitions: having a harmful effect in a gradual or subtle way
example: The most pernicious effect of the rumour was not the immediate damage it caused but the slow erosion of trust that followed for years.

definitions: spread throughout and hard to escape
example: The smell of smoke was pervasive, reaching every corner of the building even though the fire was three floors away.

definitions: having keen insight and good judgment
example: A perspicacious reader would have noticed early on that the narrator was not entirely reliable, catching inconsistencies others missed.

definitions: relevant and to the point
example: Please keep your comments pertinent to the agenda — we have limited time and several items still to cover.

definitions: calm and not easily excited
example: She was so phlegmatic about the delays that her team couldn't tell whether she had a plan or had simply stopped caring about the outcome.

definitions: brief and full of meaning
example: His pithy summary — "we spent a lot to learn a little" — captured the entire post-mortem in six words.

definitions: to calm someone down or make them less angry
example: The company tried to placate angry customers with discount vouchers, but most felt the apology itself was long overdue.

definitions: seeming reasonable or likely to be true
example: The explanation was plausible enough to satisfy a casual observer, but it collapsed the moment anyone looked closely.

definitions: an excess or overabundance
example: There is a plethora of information available online, but distinguishing the reliable from the misleading is a genuine and growing challenge.

definitions: deeply affecting or moving
example: The final scene — the old man returning to the house where he had grown up, now empty and overgrown — was quietly poignant.

definitions: dealing with things in a practical way
example: She set aside the ideological debates and took a pragmatic approach, focusing on what could actually be implemented within the existing budget.

definitions: dangerously unstable or uncertain
example: The company's financial position was precarious — one bad quarter from insolvency — though the annual report gave almost no hint of this.

definitions: to prevent something from happening
example: His prior involvement in the original decision precludes him from serving on the review committee.

definitions: showing unusually early development
example: The precocious child was reading novels at five and debating philosophy at eight, always several years ahead of her peers.

definitions: most common or influential
example: English has become the predominant language of international business and scientific publication, regardless of where that work originates.

definitions: widespread and common
example: Food insecurity is far more prevalent in urban areas than most people assume, hidden by its proximity to wealth.

definitions: in original, clean, or untouched condition
example: Despite being over two hundred years old, the document was in pristine condition — not a crease, not a fade, not a mark.

definitions: complete honesty and moral integrity
example: His reputation for probity was so strong that both parties in the dispute immediately agreed he should arbitrate.

definitions: remarkably great in size, amount, or degree
example: She had a prodigious memory — able to recall conversations from years earlier word for word — which made her formidable in any negotiation.

definitions: very deep or significant
example: The loss had a profound effect on him, reshaping his priorities in ways that were visible to everyone who knew him well.

definitions: producing much in abundance
example: She was a prolific writer, publishing four novels in five years while also producing essays, reviews, and a weekly column.

definitions: a natural tendency toward a behavior
example: He had a propensity for underestimating costs — every project he led came in over budget, always for reasons he claimed not to have anticipated.

definitions: dull, ordinary, and lacking imagination
example: The truth behind the legend was entirely prosaic: a routine business dispute that had been romanticised beyond recognition over generations.

definitions: showing care and good sense
example: Given the uncertainty in the market, the prudent course was to wait for more information before committing resources to either path.

definitions: attractively old-fashioned
example: The village was almost too quaint to seem real — thatched roofs, a cobbled square, and a pub that had not changed in fifty years.

definitions: to put an end to, especially by calming
example: It took two hours and a direct address from the director to quell the panic that had spread through the building.

definitions: frequently complaining
example: The querulous passenger complained about the seat, the temperature, the food, and the lighting — in that order, before the plane had left the gate.

definitions: representing the most perfect example of something
example: The diner was the quintessential American roadside institution — vinyl booths, bottomless coffee, and a laminated menu with something for everyone.

definitions: based on reason rather than emotion
example: He prided himself on being rational, but on closer inspection many of his supposedly logical conclusions were quietly shaped by emotion.

definitions: stubbornly resistant to authority or control
example: The recalcitrant student refused every offered compromise, determined to get exactly what he wanted or nothing at all.

definitions: to restore friendly relations or make consistent
example: It took years of difficult conversations before the two estranged brothers could reconcile and rebuild what had been lost between them.

definitions: unnecessarily repetitive or no longer needed
example: After the merger, many middle management positions were made redundant — entire layers of supervision that both organisations had duplicated.

definitions: to improve by making small changes
example: Each draft helped her refine the argument — stripping away the weaker sections and strengthening the ones that mattered most.

definitions: to assign to a lower or less important position
example: After the controversy, he was effectively relegated to a ceremonial role with no real authority, budget, or decision-making power.

definitions: to give up something willingly or by force
example: She was reluctant to relinquish control of the project even after it became clear that someone else was better placed to lead it.

definitions: deep regret for wrongdoing
example: He showed no remorse at sentencing — no acknowledgment of harm, no apology — which visibly influenced the judge's decision.

definitions: to cause to be or to provide
example: The architect's drawings rendered the proposed building in such detail that clients could see exactly what they were commissioning.

definitions: able to recover quickly from difficulty
example: The city proved resilient after the flood — businesses reopened, residents returned, and within a year it was difficult to see where the waterline had been.

definitions: reserved and not inclined to speak freely
example: He was reticent about his past, deflecting personal questions so smoothly that people rarely noticed he had said almost nothing about himself.

definitions: thorough, accurate, and strict
example: The certification process was rigorous — eighteen months of training, multiple assessments, and a final examination that fewer than forty percent passed.

definitions: strong and effective in all conditions
example: We need a more robust system — one that functions reliably even under conditions of high demand or partial hardware failure.

definitions: basic and not fully developed
example: His knowledge of the language was rudimentary — enough to order food and ask directions, but nothing beyond that.

definitions: wise and shrewd
example: The sagacious investor had seen the pattern before and quietly repositioned his portfolio months before the crash that stunned everyone else.

definitions: most noticeable or important
example: The most salient point — that the plan would cost three times the original estimate — seemed to have been buried deliberately in the report.

definitions: an official approval or penalty, depending on context
example: The committee voted to sanction the firm, imposing financial penalties that would remain in place until full compliance was demonstrated.

definitions: to examine very closely
example: Every expense claim was scrutinized by the auditors, who cross-referenced receipts, dates, and locations against the travel records.

definitions: careful, thorough, and principled
example: She was scrupulous about attribution — every fact sourced, every quote verified, never a word presented as her own that wasn't.

definitions: calm, peaceful, and untroubled
example: Despite the chaos of opening night — missed cues, a wardrobe malfunction, a forgotten prop — she remained serene backstage throughout.

definitions: the occurrence of beneficial chance discoveries
example: It was pure serendipity that brought them together — a flight delay, a shared table, and a conversation that lasted until the next morning.

definitions: not easily convinced; questioning
example: She was skeptical of the proposal from the start, pressing the team to demonstrate not just that it could work but that it actually would.

definitions: showing concern or care
example: The nurse was solicitous and attentive, checking in every hour and anticipating needs before they were expressed.

definitions: tending to cause sleep or boredom
example: The lecture was so soporific that three students had fallen asleep before the first half hour was over.

definitions: superficially plausible but actually wrong
example: The argument had a specious logic — rigorous-sounding on the surface, but built on a premise that quietly shifted halfway through.

definitions: a person who spends money in an extravagant and irresponsible way
example: His spendthrift tendencies were well known in the family: a bonus received on Friday was invariably gone by the following Tuesday.

definitions: not genuine or authentic; based on false reasoning
example: The study was later discredited as spurious, its conclusions based on data that had been selectively chosen to support a predetermined result.

definitions: loud and powerful in sound
example: Her stentorian delivery carried to the back of the hall without amplification, holding two hundred people's attention for a full hour.

definitions: enduring pain or hardship without complaint or visible emotion
example: He was stoic in the face of the diagnosis, channelling his energy immediately into planning rather than grief.

definitions: presenting a point of view in an excessively forceful or harsh way
example: Her critics called her strident, but her supporters argued she was simply unwilling to soften a message that needed to be heard.

definitions: of such excellence or beauty as to inspire awe
example: The architecture was sublime — not merely beautiful but designed to produce a sense of awe in everyone who passed through the entrance.

definitions: briefly and clearly expressed
example: His note to the team was succinct and direct: the proposal had been rejected, no revisions would be accepted, and they should move on.

definitions: a person who acts obsequiously toward someone important in order to gain advantage
example: Surrounded by sycophants who told him only what he wanted to hear, he had no accurate picture of how the project was actually going.

definitions: involving interaction between two organisms or entities to the advantage of both
example: The relationship between the clownfish and the sea anemone is symbiotic — each provides the other with protection it could not produce alone.

definitions: the combined effect of two or more elements that is greater than the sum of their parts
example: The departments had always worked in parallel rather than together; creating real synergy between them was the new director's central goal.

definitions: understood or implied without being stated directly
example: His continued attendance at the weekly meetings amounted to a tacit endorsement of the approach, even though he never said so explicitly.

definitions: reserved and saying little; not fond of talking
example: She was so taciturn in social settings that people meeting her for the first time were often surprised by how direct and forceful she was in a boardroom.

definitions: diverging from a previous course; only slightly relevant
example: The anecdote was entertaining but tangential — it said nothing about the matter at hand and consumed five minutes of limited time.

definitions: holding firmly to a purpose, belief, or course of action
example: Tenacious to the end, she pursued the case for seven years through every defeat and dismissal until the conviction was finally overturned.

definitions: sparing in the use of words; abrupt to the point of rudeness
example: His feedback on the draft was characteristically terse: "weak opening, strong middle, cut the last page entirely."

definitions: a long, angry speech of criticism or accusation
example: He unleashed a tirade at the help desk that was both unfair and counterproductive — the very people he was abusing were the ones he needed to help him.

definitions: mentally or physically inactive; sluggish
example: The torpid economy had barely grown in three years, and consumer confidence was at its lowest level in over a decade.

definitions: easy to deal with, manage, or control
example: The problem turned out to be more tractable than anyone had feared — once the root cause was identified, a solution took less than a week.

definitions: lasting only for a short time; impermanent
example: His interest in languages proved far from transient — what began as a travel necessity became a serious and lifelong pursuit.

definitions: a feeling of fear or anxiety about something that may happen
example: She opened the email with considerable trepidation, knowing it contained either the offer she had hoped for or a polite rejection.

definitions: overused and consequently lacking impact; not original
example: The advice to "believe in yourself" is so trite it has become useless — something people say when they have nothing more specific to offer.

definitions: eager or quick to argue or fight; aggressively defiant
example: The truculent union representative made clear from the opening that he was not there to reach any kind of compromise.

definitions: characterized by conflict, disorder, or confusion; not calm
example: It had been a turbulent few years for the sector — new regulations, shifting demand, and a string of high-profile failures.

definitions: offense or annoyance at something perceived as a slight
example: She took umbrage at the implication that her department was responsible for the oversight, which she felt was both unfair and unsupported by any evidence.

definitions: unable to be attacked, questioned, or defeated
example: The logic of the argument was unassailable — even those who opposed the conclusion could not find a flaw in the reasoning.

definitions: leaving no doubt; unambiguous
example: His resignation letter was unequivocal: he was leaving because of the board's decision, and he would not be returning under any circumstances.

definitions: not able to be maintained or defended against attack or objection
example: Defending a position that the data so clearly contradicted was becoming untenable, and she privately knew it.

definitions: suave, courteous, and refined in manner
example: He was unfailingly urbane — a man who could disagree with you gracefully, make you feel genuinely heard, and leave you liking him despite it.

definitions: having or showing a lack of thought or intelligence; empty
example: The press release was vacuous: three paragraphs of corporate language that said nothing at all about what had actually happened.

definitions: offering nothing that is stimulating or challenging; dull
example: The series was vapid but compulsively watchable — shallow, formulaic, and deeply comforting in its utter predictability.

definitions: showing strong feeling; forceful, passionate, or intense
example: The response from the scientific community was vehement — an open letter signed by four hundred researchers calling the findings irresponsible.

definitions: regard with great respect and reverence
example: In many cultures, age itself is venerated — the elderly are assumed to carry wisdom that can only be accumulated through decades of lived experience.

definitions: using or expressed in more words than are needed
example: The memo was so verbose that most recipients stopped reading before reaching the two action items buried on page four.

definitions: green with grass or other rich vegetation
example: After months of brown drought, the landscape was suddenly verdant — every hill a vivid, saturated green after three days of steady rain.

definitions: used for emphasis, often to qualify a metaphor; genuine or actual
example: The archive was a veritable treasure trove — thousands of letters, photographs, and documents that no scholar had ever examined.

definitions: experienced in the imagination through the feelings or actions of another person
example: He had always lived somewhat vicariously through books, experiencing adventures and places he would never encounter in his own life.

definitions: keeping careful watch for possible danger or difficulties
example: Parents were advised to remain vigilant for symptoms during the first two weeks, as some cases were slow to develop.

definitions: clear someone of blame or suspicion; show to be right or justified
example: The independent audit vindicated her completely, confirming that she had followed proper procedure at every step of the process.

definitions: filled with bitter criticism or malice
example: The exchange turned vitriolic when one panellist accused the other not just of being wrong but of acting in deliberate bad faith.

definitions: expressing or characterized by vehement opinions; loud and forceful
example: Vociferous opposition from local residents forced the council to delay the vote and hold a second round of public consultations.

definitions: deliberate and unprovoked; showing no care for consequences
example: The wanton disregard for the environment — dumping chemicals into the river for years while publicly denying it — became the defining image of the company's collapse.

definitions: feeling or showing caution about possible dangers or problems
example: After two failed partnerships, he was understandably wary of signing another agreement without independent legal review.

definitions: attractive or appealing in a fresh, innocent way
example: She had a winsome way of making requests that made people want to help — you felt better for having agreed.

definitions: having or showing a feeling of vague or regretful longing
example: He spoke wistfully about his years abroad — not with regret exactly, but with the particular tenderness reserved for things that cannot be revisited.

definitions: shriveled or wrinkled with age
example: The wizened librarian knew every book in the collection and could locate any of them from memory alone, without consulting a catalogue.

definitions: a person who performs reliable and hard work, especially in a supporting role
example: She did the yeoman work of combing through ten thousand records by hand to build the dataset the entire study depended on.

definitions: having or showing great energy or enthusiasm in pursuit of a cause or objective
example: His zealous approach to compliance — reading every regulation, flagging every ambiguity — occasionally slowed things down but had never once exposed the company to risk.

definitions: the highest point reached by a celestial body; the time at which something is most powerful
example: The decade from 1960 to 1970 represented the zenith of the space programme — more ambition, more funding, and more genuine achievement than at any other time.

definitions: a soft gentle breeze
example: On an otherwise airless afternoon, a zephyr came through the open window just long enough to lift the papers off the desk.
